changes.summary: Live Scoring no longer sits empty in the offseason. With the NFL season over, MyFantasyLeague switches its live feed off — so out of season, tapping Live Scoring in the nav dropped you on a barren "scores will appear when games begin" screen. Now, whenever the feed's dark, the page automatically replays the last completed regular season's final week: real starting lineups, the actual fantasy points every starter scored, real totals and winners, flagged with a "Sample data" badge so nobody mistakes it for a live game. The instant the season kicks off and the feed lights back up, it flips to real-time scores on its own — no toggle, no special link.

Live Scoring's matchup detail finally shows both rosters on a phone. Tap into a matchup and scroll to the player-by-player breakdown: your lineup sat on the left, the numbers ran across the middle, and the opponent's entire starting nine spilled clean off the right edge — you couldn't see the team you were playing without a sideways swipe. Rebuilt the row for small screens so both sides fit at once: your player on the left, theirs on the right, each tagged with its own color-coded position chip and score, real headshots on both. Long names now tuck to an ellipsis instead of bulldozing the score beside them, and nothing runs past the screen. The desktop layout — full names, team and game clock, projections — is untouched.

The Live Scoring scoreboard stopped running out of bounds on phones. Tap into a matchup on your phone and the big score-header — two teams, their crests, and those jumbo live totals — had been spilling off the right edge, shoving the home team's score clean off-screen behind a phantom horizontal scroll. Reworked it so each side stacks tidily (crest and name up top, the giant number underneath) and the whole card now fits the screen with everyone visible. While we were in there, the "← All matchups" back button got a little breathing room above the scorecard instead of sitting right on top of it.

What's New finally moved into the AFL homepage. The League has had that little row of recent-update cards on its front page for a while; now AFL owners get the same feed on afl-fantasy.com, browser-frame screenshots and all, pointed at the right domain. While we were in there the whole card treatment got a glow-up on both sites: the category accent colors — the little stripe and pill on each card — were a muddy, muted set that barely registered, so they got brightened to punchier greens, blues, violets and reds, and they now light up even bolder in dark mode instead of sulking. The cards themselves stopped floating shapeless against the page and picked up the same proper border and drop-shadow depth every other card on the site already wears, in both light and dark.

Wander off the map and you no longer hit a broken-looking dead end. The old 404 page had two lines of text hard-coded to near-black, so in dark mode "Page Not Found" all but disappeared into the void. The whole page got rebuilt as a Claude Schefter breaking-news alert instead: a red BREAKING banner, Schefter's mugshot on the byline, a giant 404, the headline "Sources say this page doesn't exist," and a LIVE ticker scrolling league-flavored bulletins along the bottom ("Developing: URL reportedly entered the transfer portal"). Reads perfectly in both light and dark now, and instead of making you pick a league, a single button — labeled "Back to AFL" on afl-fantasy.com and "Back to The League" on theleague.us — drops you on the homepage of whichever site you wandered off of.

The Premier League standings card on the AFL homepage stopped crowding its own headline on phones. Its "Full standings" link had been wedged into the top-right corner, sharing a cramped line with the title, while the American League playoff card right above it politely dropped its "Full bracket" link onto its own row on mobile. Now the two cards match: on phones the Premier League link tucks under the heading the same way, giving the title room to breathe.

Team logos now dress for the dark. Marks with black outlines — the Raiders shield, the Steelers hypocycloids, the Jets, North Carolina's navy interlock — used to all but vanish against a dark background, a smudge where a logo should be. In dark mode every NFL logo across the site (matchup heroes, the composite banners, roster player rows) now swaps to its dark-optimized cut, outline flipped to white so the crest actually reads, and the college logos in the Free Agents and roster player tables get the same treatment. Light mode is untouched, and the switch follows your theme toggle in real time.

The "Completed auctions report" link on the homepage auctions card now stays in one piece. It had been breaking mid-phrase — "Completed" clinging to the end of one line while "auctions report" dropped to the next — so the whole link now wraps to a fresh line together when it runs out of room.

The homepage and season heroes stopped whispering in daylight. In light mode the whole family — the What's New announcement, the weekly recap, the waiver and weekend-preview articles, the auction and cut-watch panels, the champion-crowned card, the AFL event hero and more — had faded to pale, near-white washes that all but vanished against the page, hoarding every bit of drama for dark mode. Now each wears a bold saturated card in every theme, keyed to its own color: deep league-blue for most, warm amber for the auction, alarm-red for breaking news and roster cuts, trophy-gold for a champion crowned. White headlines, on-brand glow, and the featured player bigger than ever — the daylight version finally hits as hard as the night one.

Claude Schefter is back on the beat. His scanner — the one that breaks transaction news and fires Roger's event reminders — had quietly flatlined: a duplicate line snuck into its scheduler config, which is the kind of typo the robots refuse to forgive, so the whole job got rejected before it ever woke up. Every run since had failed silently. Cleared the duplicate and the newsdesk is live again.

The player on the Cut Watch hero got a growth spurt. The bubble-player cutout — the face staring back at you while the roster-deadline countdown ticks — was sitting a little small in the corner, so we sized him up to fill the frame the way he was always meant to. More presence, same panic. Mobile is untouched.

Team defenses finally have a face in the free-agent pool. Tap a D/ST and, instead of a lonely logo staring back, the player card now leads with the unit's headliner — the Raiders' Maxx Crosby, say — his headshot on the team-color gradient and his name in the byline. It's the same trick the Free Agents spotlight already pulls up top, now brought into the detail card so a defense reads like the roster of real humans it actually is.

The "Step 1 of…" tracker at the top of the contract declaration flow stopped mumbling in the dark. That little progress label had been rendered in a dim gray that all but disappeared against the modal's dark backdrop, leaving you squinting to tell which step you were on. Bumped it to a brighter, theme-aware tone that reads cleanly in both light and dark mode — same understated label, now actually legible.

A handful of AFL links had been sneaking you across the border into The League. Biggest one: tapping "Set Lineup" while signed out bounced you to The League's login page instead of AFL's — now it sends you to the AFL sign-in and drops you right back on your lineup. Same treatment for the "View all" on the Schefter Report card and the Trade Builder buttons on the trade-offer popup, which had been pointing at The League's versions. And the header search icon, which had no AFL home to go to, steps aside on AFL until an all-pages search lands there.

The Schefter Ops dashboard finally turned the lights off. In dark mode the commissioner's rumor-mill control room had been stranded in daylight — every card stayed stark white while the page around it went dark, and the text painted on top all but vanished into the glare. Wired the whole page into the real dark theme so cards, borders, code chips and status text all dim down properly. Commish-only, so nobody else ever saw the flashbang.

The AFL Keeper Planner finally read the room. Flip on dark mode and the drag-the-line tool had stayed stubbornly, blindingly white — every keeper card, the above/below-the-line zones, the status bar and the finalize modal glowing like a lightbox on the navy page. Now the whole planner dresses in the AFL night palette: navy cards, slate borders, a green wash on the keepers you're protecting and red on the cuts. While we were in there, the Roster/Analytics/Planner toggle stopped rendering its active tab as a stark white chip, and the roster table's "Opp #" matchup badges swapped their bright pastel pills for the same muted greens and reds every other page uses after dark.

The AFL Free Agents hero gave its defenses a face. Land on the DEF tab and instead of a lonely team crest, the spotlight now leads with that unit's marquee defender — his ESPN headshot front and center, the team logo faded behind as a watermark — exactly like the offensive players already get. Each defense carries a rotating cast of its top defenders, so it opens on a different face each visit and cycles through the rest while you browse. It's the same broadcast treatment The League's board has run for weeks, now brought home to AFL.

The Free Agents hero stopped reaching for the shoebox photo. When the spotlighted player had no ESPN headshot, the panel used to fall back to a tiny, grainy MyFantasyLeague mugshot (or a gray "no photo" placeholder) — a blurry face is worse than none up in the hero. Now it simply hides the cutout and lets the team logo carry the panel, so the spotlight only ever shows a crisp headshot or a clean crest. Both The League and AFL boards get the fix.

The Cut Watch bubble player scooted over to claim the wall. He'd been floating a couple dozen pixels shy of the hero's right edge, leaving an awkward sliver of empty gradient between his shoulder and the frame. Now he's pinned flush to the edge where he belongs — same panic-inducing stare down the roster-deadline countdown, just anchored properly instead of drifting. Mobile is untouched.

The AFL Free Agents page stopped sending you to the wrong stadium. Type afl-fantasy.com/players and instead of the free-agent board you'd land on a 404 wearing The League's colors — the page had been built to render ahead of time, which quietly broke the behind-the-scenes routing that maps the clean AFL web address to the right page. Rewired it to render on request, the same way its League counterpart already does, so the AFL Free Agents board now loads exactly where it should.

Live Scoring stopped hiding the fine print after dark. When a player was booming — matching or beating his projection — his cell lights up with a green wash, but in dark mode the little "proj" number tucked underneath stayed a dim gray that all but dissolved into that glow, so the one line telling you what he was supposed to score went unreadable right when it mattered. Tinted it a legible mint that sits cleanly on the green in dark mode (and a deep green in light), so the projection reads at a glance whether or not the player's on a heater.

Live Scoring's win-probability bars finally show their true colors after dark. A bunch of teams carry near-black or deep-navy brand colors — Music City, the Magicians, Cowboy Up — and in dark mode those bars had been vanishing into the card or getting nudged to a muddy compromise, so you couldn't tell whose slice was whose. Every franchise now has a hand-picked dark-mode primary and secondary: Music City lights up in Titans sky-blue, the Magicians in bright Dark-Magician purple, and the rest in vivid, on-brand hues that read cleanly against the dark scoreboard. Light mode is untouched.

The AFL calendar got its offseason clock reset to match the real thing. The homepage had the American League draft gaveling in at 9 a.m. — it's actually a 12:30 p.m. Saturday affair — while the World Championship countdown was firing a full week early, landing on Week 16 instead of the real title week (Thursday, Dec 31). The keeper-deadline lock nudged to its true 8:45 p.m., and the "new league year" rollover moved off its stale mid-February slot to June 1, when the new AFL season actually spins up on MyFantasyLeague. Every date now lines up with the league calendar to the minute.
